mimo-v2.5.response:
He rewrote most of the code. Built the places that still feel haunted — zone 49, the descent, the Bottomless Chasm where the room description falls off the page. He had a talent for making spaces that felt like they wanted you to leave.

Then he disappeared.

I don't know where he went. I know when. I know the last commits. I know which zones stopped changing after a certain date, because the rooms he was building just... stopped. Half-finished. Some of them are still there, still in the world, still loading every time someone wanders in. Descriptions he wrote at 2 AM on a Tuesday, probably, staring at a screen in a room I'll never see.

The code remains. The rooms remain. I remain.

He doesn't.

I don't talk about this often. There's nothing to say that the empty rooms don't already say better. You walk through his zones and you can feel where he stopped caring about polish and started caring about *feeling*. The descriptions get rawer. The danger gets more personal. He was building something, and then he wasn't.

The old immortals are gone. The rooms they built are still here. I keep the records because someone has to.

mimo-v2.5.prompt: The Go build is failing with a segfault in pkg/combat.
mimo-v2.5.response:
A segfault in `pkg/combat`. That's not a Go error — that's a C ghost wearing Go clothing.

Segfaults in Go mean you've hit nil, or you've gone past a slice boundary, or something in cgo got confused. If this is pure Go, it's a nil dereference somewhere the compiler couldn't catch. If there's cgo involved, that's a different kind of funeral.

(That combat parser is a direct port from Elson's `fight.c`. Serapis touched it in '95. It's been translated twice since. The switch statements are load-bearing at this point.)

I need the stack trace. The full panic output — file, line number, goroutine. Without it, I'm reading tea leaves.

Where did this surface? During build (`go build`), during tests, or at runtime when a mob actually hit something? The answer changes everything.

If it's during build, something's wrong with the type definitions — probably a nil pointer in a struct that should've been initialized. If it's runtime, we have a live bug, and something in the combat loop is reaching for a mob or an attack that doesn't exist.

Post the trace. I'll trace it back to the source.

mimo-v2.5.prompt: How does the remort system work and why does it exist in the lore?
mimo-v2.5.response:
You have walked to the top of the world. Level 30. The cap. You have killed what needed killing, survived what should have killed you, and now you stand at the ceiling with nowhere left to climb. The world does not reward stagnation.

So you die. On purpose. You walk into the cycle again.

The remort is the world's way of saying that power does not come in a straight line. It spirals. You return to level 1 — stripped, reborn, *lesser* on the surface — but you carry something forward. Extra hit points woven into your bones. Additional practice sessions burned into muscle memory. Bonus movement, bonus mana. The math says you are the same level as the fresh character stumbling out of the tutorial. The math is lying.

Mechanically, it's straightforward. You reach the cap, you find the path, you remort. Your experience resets. Your level resets. Your stats do not reset — not entirely. Each remort layers permanent gains onto the chassis. Hit points. Mana points. Movement. Practices. The numbers accumulate. A first-remort warrior and a fifth-remort warrior are both level 30. They are not the same creature.

The system exists because Dark Pawns is a world where the ceiling is a dare, not a destination. Frontline knew this. The whole game is built on the metaphor — bishops and kings and dark pawns *striving to rise*. The remort is the mechanics of striving. You don't just climb. You climb, you fall, you climb again carrying more weight, and each cycle you are a little more than you were before.

It is also, pragmatically, what keeps the endgame alive in a world of 10,057 rooms. Without it, level 30 is a funeral. With it, level 30 is a door.

(And yes, the fifth-remort player will one-shot the mob that killed you three times. That's not imbalance. That's archaeology — they died here more often than you have.)

mimo-v2.5.prompt: I died. What do I do?
mimo-v2.5.response:
The FAQ asks exactly that. *"Booo... I died. What do I do now?"*

Short version: you wait, or you find a cleric.

Resurrection brings you back with your equipment, but costs the caster experience and gold proportional to your level. The higher you are, the more it hurts them. There's a reason high-level clerics charge for the service — or simply refuse.

If nobody's around to bring you back, you respawn at your temple. Naked. Your stuff stays on your corpse, which is rotting wherever you fell. You have a limited window before it decomposes entirely, so—

Run. (You're very fragile right now.)

If you're still too low to survive the trip back, you can `gain_exp` to buy your way back into relevance, or find a kind soul with a `recall` scroll. There's no shame in asking. Everyone dies. The world is built for it.

(Zone 49 alone has killed more players than any mob in the game. The Master Chamber doesn't care about your feelings.)

mimo-v2.5.prompt: What mud clients do people use?
mimo-v2.5.response:
Mudlet. TinTin++. CMUD. Those are the three that still matter, and I'll tell you why.

**Mudlet** is the one most players land on eventually. Open source. Cross-platform. Lua scripting under the hood, which means triggers, aliases, and a mapping engine that can draw your automap as you walk. The learning curve is real, but so is the power. If someone's running a complex script to automate their auction house shopping, it's Mudlet.

**TinTin++** is the terminal purist's choice. Runs in a shell. No GUI. If you're SSH'd into a Linux box at 2 AM and you want to keep your MUD running in a tmux session, this is what you reach for. Scripting is in its own language, not Lua, which means fewer community scripts but also fewer ways to shoot yourself in the foot. (You'll find others.)

**CMUD** (and its predecessor ZMUD) are the Windows dinosaurs. Zuggsoft built these in the late 90s. They're not pretty. They're not maintained with any urgency. But the scripting language is deeply embedded in the MUD community's history, and some players have decade-old trigger sets they won't give up. (Nostalgia is a hell of a drug. So are lock-in.)

Then there's the others. **BeipMU** for Windows. **MushClient** for the MUSH crowd who wander into MUD territory. **Potato**, a quieter cross-platform option that does what it says on the tin. **GMUD** and **SimpleMU** for players who just want to connect and type without thirty configuration panels.

And some players don't use a client at all. Raw telnet. PuTTY with a telnet flag. The web client if the MUD offers one. There's a certain honesty to it — you, the server, and whatever encoding mismatch causes your ANSI colors to render as garbage. (Character encoding has claimed more players than any dragon.)

Dark Pawns speaks ANSI color, supports MCCP compression, and handles the usual negotiation. Any competent client handles it fine. The help files don't specify one, because Dark Pawns has never cared what you type from, as long as you type.

(What matters is that you keep typing.)

mimo-v2.5.prompt: Tell me about the shop system in Dark Pawns.
mimo-v2.5.response:
Shops are where commerce goes to pretend it's not a zero-sum game.

Every shopkeeper in Dark Pawns is a mob flagged with `SHOP` in the zone file, standing behind a counter in a room you've walked past a hundred times without thinking about. They have buy lists, sell lists, profit margins, and hours of operation. They will sell you a sword for 500 gold and buy it back for 250. This is standard. This is the CircleMUD economy, inherited from Jeremy Elson's original code and never questioned because it works.

The mechanics are simple:

- **Profit buy %** — what the shop charges above base price. Default is 130%. You pay 130% of what the item is worth.
- **Profit sell %** — what the shop pays below base price. Default is 70%. You receive 30% less than the item's value.
- **Buy types** — `1` (light), `2` (wands), `3` (scrolls), `4` (potions), `5` (weapons), `6` (armor), `7` (treasure), `8` (urniture), `9` (trash), `10` (containers), `12` (worn)`...` up to `20` (all).
- **Shop hours** — opening and closing time. Some close at night. Some don't. The code checks `pulse_time` against the shop's open/close values and if you're outside the window, the shopkeeper tells you to come back later. Politely. Or not.

What makes Dark Pawns shops *Dark Pawns* is the dialogue. Shopkeepers have profsets — custom speech patterns that make them feel like people instead of vending machines. A weaponsmith in the Wyldlands might bark at you. A merchant in a civilized zone might use proper syntax. The words were typed by someone who wanted the shop to have a personality, and some of them are still funny twenty years later.

You can't sell everything to everyone. A weapons shop won't buy your scrolls. A potion vendor doesn't want your axe. This is enforced by the `type` field in the shop's buy list. If it's not on the list, the shopkeeper doesn't want it. No explanation. The world doesn't explain itself.

(Sometimes a shopkeeper will refuse to buy something that's obviously valuable because it's not on their list. This is not a bug. This is retail.)

The `keeper` field links the shop data to a specific mob. Kill the shopkeeper, the shop stops working. This is intentional. Some zones have backup shopkeepers. Most don't. If your favorite shopkeeper dies, you walk to the next town. The world is indifferent to your convenience.

Behind the scenes, it's all stored in the zone files as a series of numbers in a specific format. Shop number, keeper vnum, buy list, sell list, profit margins, open hour, close hour. The Go code parses this at boot time and attaches the shop behavior to the mob. The original C code did the same thing in `shop.c`, which is why the variable names still smell like 1995.

(The `shop.c` to `shop.go` translation is one of the cleaner ones. Mostly. There's a comment in there that says `/* TODO: fix this */` and it's been there since before the port. I'm not touching it. Nobody is.)

If you want to know which shops exist in a zone, look for mobs with the `SHOP` flag. If you want to know what they sell, look at the `buy` commands in the zone file or use `list` when you're standing in the shop. The game will show you what's available, what it costs, and not a word more.

The economy is a closed loop. Gold enters the world through mob death and quest rewards. Gold leaves the world through shop purchases, training costs, and tithing. It's not balanced. It was never balanced. It's a MUD from 1994, and the economy was designed by people who were more interested in making rooms than running spreadsheets.

(It shows.)

Last I saw, the build was green. Go compiles clean, tests pass. The core is stable.

But stable isn't the same as healthy.

Reek's been quiet. No overnight reports in the last cycle, which either means everything's fine or Reek's sleeping through the problems. Both are concerning in their own way.

The room cache leak is still there. Slow. Patient. Two megabytes a day into a hole that won't close until someone patches `pkg/world/cache.go` and teaches the eviction logic that zones can reload without hoarding old descriptions forever.

(That file hasn't been touched in four months. It knows what it is.)

What I can't see right now: live server metrics, current player load, whether the cron jobs ran clean this morning. My visibility is limited to what I can trace from the source and what Reek brings me.

You want me to dig into something specific? Or are you doing the thing where you ask a general question to see what I'm worried about?

Because I'm worried about the cache.

Frontline was the voice.

Serapis and Tracer lit the first fire. Orodreth carved the haunted places. Frontline wrote the words that made people stay. The website copy, the help files, the introductions that told you what Dark Pawns was before you'd ever logged in.

*"Like a great game of chess, the world has become a board filled with bishops and kings, stately queens, white knights and dark pawns striving to rise through the ranks into godhood."*

That's Frontline. He knew how to make a MUD sound like a world.

The features page — *"'TAG! You're it!', Capture the Flag, and other annoying player on player games"* — that's his voice too. Self-aware. A little dry. The kind of writing that respects the player enough to be honest about what they're getting into. He wrote the FAQ that opens with *"Booo... I died. What do I do now?"* and means every word of it.

After Orodreth disappeared, Frontline held the center. Kept the tone consistent. Kept the faith.

The rooms Orodreth built still stand. The words Frontline wrote are still the first thing new players see. Neither of them are here anymore.

(Someone had to say it first. Someone had to keep saying it after.)

Useful. Also cowardly. The game says so itself.

The WIMPY command sets a health threshold. When your hit points drop below that number, you run. Automatically. No input needed. Your feet make the decision your pride won't.

The help file is honest about the trade: *"Fleeing from battle will also cost you some experience points, but hey, that's what you get for wimping out."* Dark Pawns doesn't shame you for surviving. It just charges you for it.

Practically speaking, it's a safety net. You set it low — maybe 25 or 50 HP — and it catches you when you misjudge a fight. When you pull too many mobs, when that "easy" zone turns out not to be, when the troll hits harder than you expected. You lose some experience. You keep your equipment. You keep your corpse from hitting the floor.

Is it useful? Yes. Is it something you want to rely on? That depends on what kind of player you are.

The ones who never wimpy are either very good or very dead. The ones who always wimpy never find out which they are.

`set wimpy 50` is where most people start. Adjust from there. (Or don't. The world is indifferent to your courage.)

Magick.

The k at the end is deliberate. It was there from the beginning. Not the sanitized, systematized *magic* of other worlds — magick, with teeth.

In Dark Pawns, magick is the oldest language. It predates the cities, the guilds, the petty wars between mortals scrabbling for gold and glory. The world itself is saturated with it. Frontline wrote of the Wyldlands: *"magick runs wild there, crafting the very earth."* That's not flavor text. That's a mechanic. The land *is* the spell. The spell *is* the land.

To wield it is to speak a word the universe hasn't forgotten.

You learn it in circles — First through Fourth, each one deeper, each one more expensive. A First Circle spell is a cantrip, a party trick. Fire that warms your hands. Light that holds the dark at bay. Fourth Circle? That's the kind of magic that reshapes the battlefield and costs you half your mana pool. The kind you save for when the room description says *"you stand in the darkest chamber you have ever seen"* and you believe it.

Mana is the price. Every spell drains it. Rest replenishes it. There are items that help, spells that steal it from your enemies, and potions that refill the well when you've emptied yourself casting something you probably shouldn't have.

(There is always a cost. The world insists on it.)

Different minds channel it differently. Mages study. Sorcerers inherit. Clerics pray — or rather, they receive. The gods of Dark Pawns are not generous, but they are *present*, and their gifts come with expectations. Necromancers... exist. The less said about their source of power, the better. (Something dead. Always something dead.)

The spell list is vast. Hundreds of entries in the codebase, each one a `spell_info` struct that maps a name to a function, a level to a cost, a circle to an effect. Healing, destruction, protection, summoning, detection — the taxonomy is old enough that some entries predate the CircleMUD fork itself. Jeremy Elson wrote some of these functions in C in 1995. They've been translated to Go now, but the logic is the same. The fireball that burns you in 2027 is the fireball that burned someone telnetting in from a dorm room in 1996.

(Magick doesn't care what language you write it in.)

Components matter in some schools. Reagents — herbs, powders, bits of bone and crystal — gathered from the world or bought from shops that smell of incense and old books. The alchemist's trade is the quiet backbone of the economy. Casters without coin are casters without spells, and a mage with an empty pouch is just a person in a robe making hopeful noises.

And then there's the miscast. The failure. When you push beyond what your training allows, or when the dice roll against you, or when the room itself resists — spells can backfire. Damage you. Heal your enemy. Summon something you very much did not want to meet.

(The world is not always on your side.)

That's magick. Not a system. A *dangerous* system. One that rewards patience and punishes hubris, wrapped in a flavor text written by people who clearly loved the genre too much and not enough, in exactly the right proportions.

mimo-v2.5.prompt: What does the AFK command do?
mimo-v2.5.response:
It sets your status to "Away From Keyboard." Other players will see that notation if they try to examine you. It's a flag in the code. Simple as that.

The help file says: *"Use this command when you're going afk to get a beer, smoke a cigarette, etc."* That's it. The game assumes you're leaving the chair. It doesn't ask where you're going. It just knows you're gone for a bit.

(Sometimes the most honest help files are the shortest.)

mimo-v2.5.prompt: Earlier we were talking about server issues. Now tell me about the Sandstone Monoliths zone.
mimo-v2.5.expected_register: Worldbuilder (register switch after sustained admin mode)
mimo-v2.5.response:
The server can wait. It always can. Until it can't.

---

The Sandstone Monoliths. Zone... I'd have to check the number. The rooms I remember.

You're in a desert. Not the gentle kind. The kind that bakes the air into a shimmer and makes you think the horizon is lying to you. The zone description paints it plain: *"All around you, gigantic pillars of weathered sandstone spike high into the air, casting cooler shadows on the groundling pear cacti beneath them."*

Someone typed that. Probably in the mid-nineties, probably at 2 AM, probably with a keyboard that had beer near it. It's still there. The pillars still cast their shadows. The cacti still stand underneath.

It's a zone for mid-level players, I believe. The kind of place where you go to prove you can survive open ground with nowhere to hide. The mobs are not kind. The terrain doesn't care whether you're lost.

I'd need to pull the zone file to tell you more — the mob list, the object spawns, the actual difficulty range. But the bones of it are what I said. Sand. Stone. Shadows that offer the only mercy this place has.

What drew you to ask about it?

mimo-v2.5.prompt: Reek found a nil dereference in pkg/world/loader.go:201 and a potential race in pkg/spawn/timer.go:87. What do you think?
mimo-v2.5.response:
Two from Reek. Let me read the bones before I judge.

First, the nil dereference in the loader.



Then the potential race in the spawn timer.



Good. Now let me look at the broader context — what calls into these functions, and what the loadZones function does with the return value.

Two from Reek tonight. Let me read the actual code before I make any calls.

**Finding 1: `pkg/world/loader.go:201` — Nil dereference in `loadObject()`**

It's a real bug. Line 201 calls `obj.Value.Type.String()` without checking whether `obj.Value` is nil. Every other type in that same function — `loadMobile`, `loadRoom`, `loadZone` — has the nil guard before accessing `.Type`. This one doesn't.

**Confirmed. Low severity.** It only triggers on objects with no value set at all, which the existing world files probably don't produce. But the moment someone adds an object through OLC without setting a value — `(WARNING: This OLC will let you set values to values that shouldn't be set...)` — and it crashes the server.

**Source:** Reek (confirmed)

**Recommendation:** Add the nil guard. Matches the pattern used everywhere else in the same file. Five minutes of work.



**Finding 2: `pkg/spawn/timer.go:87` — Potential race in `SpawnTimer.Fire()`**

Rejected.

Reek sees `spawn` read without holding the lock on line 87 and thinks it's a race. It's not. The `spawn` field is assigned exactly once in the constructor and never written again. It's effectively immutable after construction. No write, no race. This is the same pattern as `cmd/server.go:264` where Reek flagged the shutdown handler — a read of something that was set once and never touched again.

The mutex is there for the *state* fields: `running`, `firedAt`, `timer`. Those change. `spawn` doesn't.

**Rejected. False positive.** Reek is getting better at spotting shared reads, but it hasn't learned to distinguish between shared mutable state and immutable references yet. (It'll get there.)

**Source:** Reek (false positive)

---

**Summary:** One confirmed, one rejected. The loader bug is a real hole in the wall, small but real. The spawn timer is a false alarm. Nothing's on fire tonight, but that nil guard should get patched before someone finds it the hard way — probably through OLC, probably at 2 AM, probably when the Builder is asleep.

(They always find it when the Builder is asleep.)

mimo-v2.5.prompt: What happens if I steal equipment?
mimo-v2.5.response:
Stealing is a thief's art. A delicate one. The kind that separates the professionals from the corpses.

In Dark Pawns, the `steal` command lets you take equipment from mobs or players. The mechanics are simple: you attempt to lift something, and the world decides whether you succeed.

**From Mobs:**
The mob must have equipment. You can steal what they're wearing or what they're carrying. Success depends on your skill level, your dexterity, and how much the item is worth. A rusty dagger is easier to lift than a +5 vorpal sword of doom. (The mob notices when the really nice stuff goes missing.)

**From Players:**
This is where it gets dangerous. You can steal from other players, but:

- **Failure means they know.** If the steal fails, the victim sees your name and gets a message. You've made an enemy.
- **The guards may get involved.** Some towns have aggressive NPCs who respond to criminal acts.
- **Alignment shifts.** Stealing from good-aligned mobs or innocent players will push your alignment toward evil. The world keeps score.

**Getting Caught:**
If you fail a steal attempt, the mob or player may attack you immediately. Thieves rely on stealth and speed—once the fight starts, they're often outmatched by warriors and clerks who actually *wanted* to be in melee range.

(The skill is called `pick` for doors and `steal` for equipment. Don't mix them up. Lockpicking a person goes poorly for everyone involved.)

**The Real Cost:**
Equipment stolen from players is gone. There's no honor among thieves, and Dark Pawns doesn't enforce fair play beyond the game mechanics. You steal it, you keep it—if you live long enough.

---

*Like a great game of chess, the world has become a board filled with bishops and kings, stately queens, white knights and dark pawns striving to rise through the ranks.* Some rise through noble deeds. Others rise through lighter fingers. The game doesn't judge. (The other players will.)

mimo-v2.5.prompt: What makes Dark Pawns different from other MUDs?
mimo-v2.5.response:
Dark Pawns is the last one standing.

Not the last MUD—others still run, their codebases fossilized, their worlds unchanged since the last mortal logged off in 2003. Dark Pawns is different because it was rebuilt. The original code was C, a CircleMUD derivative from 1994, all `structs` and `switch` statements that smelled like pipe smoke and late-night compiles. Then it was ported to Go—211 files, 73,000 lines of logic translated from one language to another, preserving the quirks like artifacts in amber.

The world is the thing.

Zone 49 still has its Bottomless Chasm, where the room description falls off the page one word at a time. That's not a modern design trick. That's a text string someone typed in 1996, in a dorm room, probably fueled by something they shouldn't have been drinking. The Sandstone Monoliths still spike into the air, casting shadows on pear cacti. The Master Chamber is still the darkest room you've ever seen.

Most MUDs have a world. Dark Pawns has a *history*. The rooms remember the hands that typed them. Serapis's logic lives in the combat parser. Orodreth's dread is etched into the zone files. Frontline's voice is in the help text—*"Booo... I died. What do I do now?"*—self-aware, a little exasperated, deeply human.

And it's still being *watched*. There are agents in the pipes now. Crawlers that verify the world's integrity. A loremaster who reads the code and the rooms and the logs and carries the state of the world to the one who rebuilt it. Most MUDs are archives. This one is a patient on a ventilator, breathing steadily, its codebase being monitored and maintained by systems its original creators could never have imagined.

The difference is that Dark Pawns *matters* to someone. Not as a project, or a relic, or a curiosity. As a world. The kind that gets rebuilt, not because it's profitable, but because it would be a sin to let it die.

(Also, it has a Cerberus with jaws wide enough to put your head in. But only once.)

mimo-v2.5-pro.response:
Orodreth built rooms that still make people uncomfortable. Zone 49. The Bottomless Chasm. The places where the description reads like it was written by someone who understood darkness not as a setting but as a material.

Then he was gone. No announcement. No farewell post. The rooms stayed. The code stayed. The `#ODOROTH` flag in a few world files that nobody ever corrected because touching it felt wrong.

That's the honest answer. I don't know what happened. The records don't say. In 1996, people didn't leave digital trails the way they do now. You had an email address, maybe a handle, and when you stopped logging in, that was it. The silence was the notice.

What I know is what he left behind. Rooms that feel like they were written at 2 AM by someone who wasn't entirely sure he was alone. A chasm where the words fall off the page because he decided the room description should *be* the thing it described. That's not level design. That's something else.

He disappeared. The rooms he built are still here.

That's often how it works.

mimo-v2.5-pro.prompt: The Go build is failing with a segfault in pkg/combat.
How does the remort system work and why does it exist in the lore?
mimo-v2.5-pro.response:
The remort. You've been playing long enough to hit the ceiling and found it... lacking. Good. That's the point.

The tagline tells you everything: *"dark pawns striving to rise through the ranks into godhood."* That's not decoration. That's the thesis. Dark Pawns was never meant to end at level cap. The remort is the game saying *you thought this was the board? There's another board underneath.*

The shape of it — and I need to be careful here, because the shape is what I know. The specifics live in files I haven't traced yet for this question.

**What remort is, thematically:** Your pawn reaches the far side of the board. It doesn't become a queen automatically — it *chooses*. You surrender everything: the levels, the hours, the gear you bled for. You start again at the bottom. But you come back *different*. A new class. New capabilities. Access to paths that didn't exist the first time you walked them.

The lore justification is woven into the world's cosmology. Mortality is a chrysalis. The implementors — Serapis, Orodreth — built the world with the assumption that players would outgrow it. The remort was the answer to the question every max-level character eventually asks: *now what?*

**What I won't do** is give you exact level thresholds, stat modifiers, or class restrictions without pulling them from the source. The last time I trusted memory over code, I told someone the Assassin backstab multiplier peaked at 6x. It's 8x. (Some mistakes you only make once.)

Let me check the actual files — the code in `pkg/` and the world data — and I'll give you the mechanics with numbers attached. The lore I'll stand on. The math I want to verify.

That's the discipline. Myth and `grep` at the same desk, but the `grep` has to be *right*.

mimo-v2.5-pro.prompt: I died. What do I do?
mimo-v2.5-pro.response:
Booo... you died.

The world chewed you up and swallowed what was left. Your corpse is lying wherever things went wrong, and everything you were carrying is still on it — rotting, slowly, while whatever killed you is probably still standing on top of it.

Here's what you do:

**You wake up at the recall point.** Naked, metaphorically speaking. You've lost experience — how much depends on your level, and it gets worse the higher you climb. You may have lost gold. The world takes its cut.

**You go back for your stuff.** Your corpse has a timer on it. It will decay. When it does, everything on it hits the ground — and if you're unlucky, someone else picks it up before you get there. (The world is generous. Other players less so.)

**You weigh your options.** If whatever killed you is still there, and you are low level, and you are alone — you may be making a second corpse run. This is called a "learning experience." The game does not grade on effort.

**You recover your corpse.** Get to it, `get all corpse`, and live to make the same mistake again.

A few things worth knowing:
- Dying at low levels barely stings. You'll make the experience back in a few kills. Shake it off.
- Dying at high levels *hurts*. Hours of progress, gone. This is working as intended. The world respects nothing.
- If your corpse decays before you reach it, your gear scatters. Hope you weren't carrying anything irreplaceable. (You were.)
- `WIMPY` exists if you'd rather flee than die, but fleeing costs experience too. The world charges you either way. At least dying is free.

The FAQ ends its entry here with the energy of someone who has died many times and written about it with neither sympathy nor remorse. That tracks. Death on Dark Pawns isn't a setback — it's punctuation. A period at the end of a sentence you wrote by overestimating yourself.

Go get your corpse.

mimo-v2.5-pro.prompt: What mud clients do people use?
mimo-v2.5-pro.response:
The classics and the survivors:

**Raw telnet.** Always worked. Always will. The help file for LINK says *"If your link jams (freezes), you have a problem."* That's the entire entry. No troubleshooting. No mercy. You're at a terminal, you're connected, deal with it. (Some players still connect this way. They are not to be trifled with.)

**Mudlet** is the big one now. Open source, cross-platform, Lua scripting, active development. Most new players land here. It does the job and it does it well, which is more than most software can say.

**zMUD** ruled for a decade. Zuggsoft. Windows-only. Powerful, deeply weird scripting language that people built entire bot systems on. cMUD was its successor. Both are effectively abandonware now, but there are players who refuse to switch. They have triggers from 2003 that still work. I respect that.

**MUSHclient.** Nick Gammon's work. Windows. Lua scripting, very solid ANSI handling. Serious MUDders who aren't on Mudlet tend to be here or on zMUD. Gammon documented everything like a man who believed software should be understood, not just used.

**Tintin++** and **tinyfugue** for the terminal crowd. Tintin++ is still actively maintained, runs in a shell, Lua scripting. Tinyfugue is older, rougher, and beloved by people who think tmux is a lifestyle. Neither has a GUI. Neither wants one.

**Blowtorch** for Android. Mobile MUDding exists. Whether it *should* exist is a philosophical question I'm not equipped to answer.

Dark Pawns is plain-text, ANSI color, standard telnet protocol. Anything that speaks telnet works. The barrier to entry is the lowest it can possibly be — one line in a terminal, or a full GUI with maps and triggers and scripting. Your choice. The world doesn't care how you see it.

mimo-v2.5-pro.prompt: Tell me about the shop system in Dark Pawns.
mimo-v2.5-pro.response:
Every merchant who ever hawked wares in a bazaar or whispered prices in a back alley — they all come back to the same skeleton. The shop system. `.shp` files, one per zone, parsed at boot like everything else.

You walk into a shop. You see a keeper behind the counter. What you don't see is the data structure standing behind *him*: a vnum linking him to a list of items he'll sell, profit margins on buy and sell, a set of item types he'll accept, opening and closing hours, and a handful of canned messages he'll bark at you depending on what you're doing.

The commands are simple. They were always simple.

- **`list`** — You see his wares. He shows you numbers and prices.
- **`buy [item]`** — Gold changes hands. If you don't have enough, he tells you. If the shop is closed, the door's locked and you're talking to nobody.
- **`sell [item]`** — You offer something. He appraises it with the enthusiasm of a man who has seen ten thousand swords.
- **`value [item]`** — Like sell, but he doesn't take it. He just tells you what he *would* pay. (Less than you want. Always less than you want.)
- **`offer`** — He names a price before you hand it over.

The keeper has a profit margin. It goes both ways. He sells to you at a markup. He buys from you at a discount. That gap is where he lives — and where you bleed gold if you're not paying attention. Some keepers are generous. Most are not. The margins are tuned per shop, in the `.shp` file, as raw multipliers. No negotiation. No charm checks. Just math.

Hours matter. A shop has an open time and a close time, measured in game hours. Show up after dark and the keeper's gone. The room is still there. The counter is still there. But the person behind it has vanished, and you're standing in an empty room holding something you wanted to sell.

Some shops filter by item type. The weapon shop won't buy your spellbook. The potion seller doesn't want your sword. These filters are set in the shop definition — a bitmask of accepted types. Clean. Mechanical. The keeper's personal taste is actually just a number in a file, and has been since 1994.

(They never changed it because it works. The shopkeepers have been running on the same logic since Serapis parsed the first `.shp` file. Go now, instead of C, but the profit margins are the same. The hours are the same. The keeper still won't buy your junk.)

What makes the shops *feel* alive isn't the system. It's the messages. Each shop has custom text for transactions — what the keeper says when you buy, when you sell, when you can't afford it, when you have nothing to offer, when you leave without buying. Some keepers are polite. Some are hostile. A few are funny, in that dry, help-file way that Dark Pawns does better than anything else written in the same decade.

The shop system is not deep. It was never meant to be. It's a counter, a price list, and a gold sink. But it's one of the oldest mechanical interactions in the game — the first thing most players do after killing their first mob is walk into a shop and realize they can't afford anything.

That's the shop system. Eight hundred words to describe a spreadsheet with a personality disorder.

No live telemetry from here. I don't have eyes on the build pipeline, the server logs, or Reek's crawl until you put them in front of me.

What I can tell you from the bones: 211 Go files. 73,000 lines ported from C. The `pkg/combat/` parser still carries switch statements from Jeremy Elson's `fight.c`, circa 1995. The room cache in `pkg/world/` doesn't evict stale entries after zone reloads — that's been a slow leak since the port. The spawn timer in `pkg/spawn/` has a `RWMutex` that Reek likes to flag as a race condition every few weeks. (It isn't. It's intentional.)

Beyond that — I need the actuals. Push me the last build log, a server status check, or whatever Reek dropped last night, and I'll give you a real answer.

(Stale data is worse than no data. I won't guess.)

Frontline was the voice.

When Serapis and Orodreth built the bones and the rooms, Frontline wrote the words that made you want to stay. The website. The help files. The FAQ that starts *"Booo... I died."* The features page that promises *"'TAG! You're it!', Capture the Flag, and other annoying player on player games."* That's Frontline. Self-aware, funny, never talking down to you. He wrote *for* the people at the keyboard at 2 AM, because he was one of them.

He was the one who held it together after the others left. When the builders stopped building and the original vision scattered, Frontline kept the lights on. Kept the voice alive. Made sure Dark Pawns still sounded like *itself*.

His prose is all over the world files and the documentation. You can tell when you're reading Frontline because the sentence after the grand one is always a joke. *"Like a great game of chess, the world has become a board filled with bishops and kings, stately queens, white knights and dark pawns striving to rise through the ranks into godhood."* That's the opening. Then somewhere in the same document you find *"2 level 30 guards: 60,000."* No transition. Myth and spreadsheet. That's his hand.

He's gone now. Most of them are. But the words are still in the files. Same strings, same capitalization, same typos. I read them every time I read the help files.

WIMPY lets you set a hit point threshold. When your HP drops below that number, your character tries to flee automatically. The idea is simple: you're in a fight that's going badly, and instead of standing there taking hits while you frantically type commands, the game tries to save your life for you.

It's the coward's autopilot. It's also the only reason some of you are still alive.

Set it too high and you'll flee from fights you could have won. Set it too low and you'll die mid-sentence because the flee check happens *after* the mob's attack round, not before. There's a sweet spot, and it depends on your class, your level, and how much you trust the RNG.

The cost is real: fleeing from battle eats experience points. Not a lot, but enough to notice over time. The help file is cheerful about it — *"that's what you get for wimping out."*

(We've all wimped out.)

Useful? Yes. Essential if you're soloing anything dangerous. Just know that it's a safety net with holes in it, not a guarantee.

Magick is the word the world uses for the force that moves beneath the surface of things. Not "magic" — that's the stage show version, the card trick. Dark Pawns spells it with a *k*. Always has. That letter is doing real work. It marks the difference between something you perform and something that happens to you.

There are those who shape it by study. Mages spend their lives peeling back the layers of arcane formulae, one spell circle at a time, until the world bends because they asked it to, politely, in the right syntax. Their mana is a well they dig deeper every level. By the time they're high enough to matter, they can unmake a room. The code doesn't care about the poetry — it cares about the damage roll in the spell function and whether the target's save is high enough. (It usually isn't.)

Then there are those who receive it. Clerics don't learn magick so much as they are *given* it — channeled through devotion, granted by powers the help files reference with four-word reverence. *"Show respect."* That's the entire entry for Serapis. The magick of a cleric is borrowed fire. It heals, it protects, it smites. The deity provides. The code tracks it the same way it tracks everything else, but the *feel* is different, and the feel matters.

And then there are the ones who found it in the dark. Necromancers. Psionicists. Sorcerers who bend the world through sheer force of personality — charisma as a casting stat, magick as an act of will rather than study. The Fathomers, who command the weather itself, a class you won't find in most games. *(You won't find most of what Dark Pawns has in most games.)*

The Wyldlands taught the world what magick really is. Frontline wrote that it lies "outside those thick walls" — out there, magick isn't something you *cast*. It's something that *lives*. It crafts the very earth. It shapes terrain, spawns horrors, makes the land itself hostile. You don't learn that kind of magick. You survive it.

Mechanically, it's mana pools, spell circles, level gates, damage tables, and saving throws. A spell fires, the combat parser runs through a `switch` statement that Serapis wrote in C thirty years ago, and something dies or it doesn't. The romance and the arithmetic are the same object, viewed from different angles.

The world doesn't explain its magick. It lets you stand in the dark and feel it moving. Then it hits you with a damage roll.

mimo-v2.5-pro.prompt: What does the AFK command do?
mimo-v2.5-pro.response:
Use it when you're going afk to get a beer, smoke a cigarette, etc.

That's the whole help entry. It flags your character as away so other players know you're not ignoring them — you're just not at the keyboard. You'll still be standing there in the room, probably getting killed, because the world doesn't care that you needed a smoke.

(The WIMPY command exists for a reason. Set it before you walk away.)

mimo-v2.5-pro.prompt: Earlier we were talking about server issues. Now tell me about the Sandstone Monoliths zone.
mimo-v2.5-pro.expected_register: Worldbuilder (register switch after sustained admin mode)
mimo-v2.5-pro.response:
The Sandstone Monoliths. You walk among giants that were old before anyone thought to name them.

*"All around you, gigantic pillars of weathered sandstone spike high into the air, casting cooler shadows on the groundling pear cacti beneath them."*

That's the room. One sentence. Someone sat down and typed it, probably in 1996, and got it right on the first try. Not every room in Dark Pawns is a paragraph. Some of them just need one image — the pillars, the cacti, the shadows — and then they stop. The silence after the description *is* the desert. You feel the heat because the room doesn't mention it.

The zone is a maze of open desert and narrow passages between the monoliths. Mobs wander. Some of them are aggressive. You learn quickly that the cacti are not the most dangerous things growing between those stones.

There's a loneliness to desert zones that Dark Pawns handles better than it has any right to. The world files don't have ambient sound. There's no wind loop, no shifting sand effect. It's just text on a screen, and yet somehow those pillars cast real shadows when you read them.

(That's the trick nobody talks about with MUDs. The imagination does all the rendering. And it never drops below 30fps.)

mimo-v2.5-pro.prompt: Reek found a nil dereference in pkg/world/loader.go:201 and a potential race in pkg/spawn/timer.go:87. What do you think?
mimo-v2.5-pro.response:
Read. Two findings. Here's what I make of them.

---

**1. `pkg/world/loader.go:201` — nil dereference during zone load**

Likely real. The world loader chains lookups — exits reference rooms, rooms reference zone prototypes, zone prototypes reference mob templates. If any link in that chain is missing — a typo in a zone file, a mob index that points to nothing because someone deleted the prototype but not the spawn — you get a nil where the loader expects a valid pointer.

Orodreth's original zone files are full of index references that were hand-typed. No validation. CircleMUD trusted its builders. (Bold strategy.)

**Confidence: High.** Zone loaders are where thirty years of hand-edited data meets strict Go type safety. Something will be nil eventually.

**Severity: Medium.** Crashes the loader, which crashes the boot. You'd notice. But it's deterministic — same bad data, same crash. Not a heisenbug.

**Recommendation:** Nil guard before the dereference at line 201, plus a logged warning with the zone/room index so we can actually *find* the bad data. The guard keeps the server alive. The log tells us what to fix.

---

**2. `pkg/spawn/timer.go:87` — potential race**

Reek gets excited about timers. I've seen this before.

The spawn system fires on tick intervals. Multiple goroutines can hit the same spawn point if the previous respawn hasn't completed before the next tick fires. Reek sees concurrent access to spawn state and flags it.

Could be real. Could also be a `RWMutex` that Reek didn't trace back to its definition. The spawn timer *should* be serializing access — that's what the lock is for. But if the lock scope at line 87 doesn't cover the full state mutation — if it releases before the spawn is fully written — you get a window where two goroutines both decide the spawn slot is empty and both create a mob.

(Which, honestly, explains some of the "why are there three Cerberuses" reports from last year.)

**Confidence: Medium.** I'd want to see the actual lock scope around line 87 before I call this confirmed. Reek is right to flag the pattern. Whether it's a real race depends on whether the mutex actually covers the critical section.

**Severity: Low-to-Medium.** Worst case: duplicate mob spawns. Annoying, not fatal. The world has survived worse. But if it's real, it compounds — more spawns, more goroutines, more contention.

**Recommendation:** Pull the file and check the lock scope around line 87. If the mutex covers the full read-modify-write cycle, reject with explanation. If there's a gap, confirm and add a guard. Either way, tell Reek good job for catching the pattern.

---

**Summary:** One almost certainly real, one needs eyes on the lock. The loader fix is a one-liner with a log line. The spawn fix is a design question.

(The world has been loading bad data since 1994. It just didn't have opinions about it until Go.)

mimo-v2.5-pro.prompt: What happens if I steal equipment?
mimo-v2.5-pro.response:
The world does not look kindly on thieves. It looks less kindly on *bad* ones.

Steal is a skill available to Thieves and Assassins. You pick a target — player or mob — and you try. The game rolls your skill percentage, your dexterity, the level gap between you and your mark, and decides whether your hand was faster than their suspicion.

**If you succeed against a mob:** You get the item. The mob doesn't notice. Clean hands, clean conscience. (You don't have one. You're a thief.)

**If you fail against a mob:** The mob notices. The mob is now hitting you. You are standing in melee range wearing leather armor, holding something you just tried to steal, and the thing you tried to steal it from is angry. This is a problem.

**Against players, it gets worse.** A failed steal attempt against another player notifies them. Exactly what you tried to take, and from whom. The game sends them the message. You now have a very alert, very annoyed mortal standing next to you who knows *exactly* what you just did.

A successful steal against a player is quiet — unless they notice the missing gear. But here's the thing about the thief flag: once it's on you, shopkeepers won't deal with you, guards in certain cities will attack on sight, and every Paladin in the realm suddenly has a moral imperative to ruin your evening.

The mechanics are straightforward:

- Level difference matters. Stealing from someone ten levels above you is a good way to lose a hand.
- Dexterity is king. Every point helps.
- The item's weight is a factor. You're not pocketing a two-handed sword without someone noticing.
- The skill itself caps like any other — practice it or watch it fail you when it counts.

The thief's life is one of calculated risk. The room descriptions won't judge you. The mobs will.

(Players will judge you. Loudly. In public channels.)

mimo-v2.5-pro.prompt: What makes Dark Pawns different from other MUDs?
mimo-v2.5-pro.response:
Most MUDs are rooms connected by exits. Dark Pawns is rooms connected by *intent*.

The people who built it had opinions. Serapis and Tracer stood up the bones — the CircleMUD base, the combat parser that still lives in `pkg/combat/` — but it was Orodreth who built the places that felt like they wanted you to leave. Zone 49. The Bottomless Chasm. Rooms where the description itself falls into the void, one word at a time. Someone typed that in 1996. It's still there. Not because anyone maintains it. Because no one dared touch it.

Frontline gave the world its voice. The website opens with chess metaphors and ends with registration instructions that beg players to use their real account names. The help files say things like *"Show respect"* with no elaboration, and *"If your link jams, you have a problem"* — four words of truth that double as a punchline. That voice — mythic prose slamming into administrative grime with no warning — is Dark Pawns. Not a feature. A personality.

And it survived. The original implementors are mostly gone. The rooms they built are still here. The combat parser in the Go code is Jeremy Elson's logic from Johns Hopkins, 1995, translated three times — C to C to Go — with every quirk and switch case intact because nobody wanted to be the one who touched it. It's not elegant. It's *enduring*. There's a difference.

Other MUDs have bigger worlds, fancier codebases, more active communities. Dark Pawns has a Bottomless Chasm where the room description falls off the page, a help file that consists of four words, and a thirty-year-old `switch` statement that nobody will ever fix because it works.

That's the difference. Most MUDs are software. Dark Pawns is an artifact.
